channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The World Cup Gives Kansas City a Rare Turn in the Global Spotlight
item.description: The tournament brings unique challenges and upsides for Kansas City, the smallest U.S. metro area to host matches.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Bernadett Szabo/Reuters
item.media:description: Argentina fans celebrate after the match in Kansas City, Mo., on Tuesday.

item.title: Merlin, the Duck in a Mexico World Cup Team Jersey, Finds Unexpected Fame
item.description: The feathered pet has waddled his way into the pantheon of famous animals associated with the soccer tournament.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Marco Ugarte, via Associated Press

item.title: 12 Injured at Cultus Lake Water Park, Possibly by Electricity, in British Columbia
item.description: The children sustained serious but not life-threatening injuries at Cultus Lake Waterpark in British Columbia, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: CBC
item.media:description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police said they responded on Monday morning to a report of multiple injuries at Cultus Lake Waterpark, in Cultus Lake, B.C., about 65 miles southeast of Vancouver.

item.title: ‘Hockey Night in Canada,’ a Saturday Night Ritual on the CBC, Is No More
item.description: The longtime over-the-air telecast will no longer be free after the national broadcaster, and Rogers Sportsnet, the N.H.L. rights-holder, could not agree on a sub-licensing deal.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Todd Korol for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Edmonton Oilers and the Calgary Flames during a game in Edmonton, Alberta. The “Battle of Alberta” was always a marquee Saturday night game on “Hockey Night in Canada.”

item.title: War Loomed Over Past World Cups, Too
item.description: Here’s a look back at times when international conflicts seeped into soccer’s biggest tournament.
item.dc:creator: Qasim Nauman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranian fans during the World Cup match between Iran and New Zealand at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, Calif., on Monday.

item.title: Rope Jumper Falls to Her Death After Operators Fail to Secure Harness
item.description: Harrowing videos of the incident in Brazil showed the woman lifted over the heads of two instructors, her arms spread like wings, as they threw her off a bridge.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:credit: Google Maps
item.media:description: The Ponte do Esqueleto, or Skeleton Bridge, a popular spot for rope jumping about 100 miles northwest of São Paulo, Brazil.

item.title: How Quebec Left the Church and Became the World Leader in Assisted Dying
item.description: A society once strongly Roman Catholic has rejected the church’s prohibition on euthanasia, with many instead viewing control over one’s death as an individual right.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi and Hubert Hayaud
item.media:description: In Lanaudière, 13 of 100 people end their lives through medically assisted dying.

item.title: Inside Colombia’s Never-Ending Drug War
item.description: Ten years after a landmark peace deal, armed groups are wreaking deadly violence, blending jungle combat with new drone warfare.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Federico Rios
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: FARC guerrillas in Norte de Santander, Colombia, where they are clashing with another armed group, the ELN.

item.title: Singer Oliver Tree Presumed Dead After Helicopter Crash in Brazil
item.description: The two helicopters crashed in Rio de Janeiro on Sunday, killing at least six people, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Tercio Teixeira/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Los helicópteros se estrellaron el domingo sobre un concesionario de autos en Río de Janeiro.

item.title: Curaçao Is the Smallest Country Ever to Reach the World Cup
item.description: Competing for the first time, Curaçao is brimming with joy. Most of the Caribbean nation’s team was born and raised in the Netherlands, but residents say the players represent them.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Luis Antonio Rojas
item.media:description: Soccer players and artists at a celebration this month in Curaçao, a Caribbean island and constituent country of the Kingdom of the Netherlands.

item.title: In Venezuela, a 91-Year-Old Weaver Sticks to Traditions
item.description: Though electric machines are now standard, the Venezuelan weaver Margarita Mora has clung to a mix of ancestral Indigenous and Spanish practices to create surprisingly modern work.
item.dc:creator: Silvia Benedetti and Andrea Hernández
item.media:credit: Andrea Hernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Top Haitian Security Official Kidnapped
item.description: A security expert who had recently become chief of staff to the new defense minister was abducted, the latest example of violence gripping the country.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Odelyn Joseph/Associated Press
item.media:description: Cars burned by armed gangs in downtown Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in January.

item.title: 5 Children Are Killed After Van and S.U.V. Collide in Rural Ontario
item.description: The children — four girls and one boy — were among 10 people in the van at the time of the crash. An infant was seriously injured, the police said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: A Tren de Aragua Leader Is Killed in a Joint Strike, U.S. and Venezuela Say
item.description: A strike this week in Venezuela killed a gang leader known as Niño Guerrero who was wanted in the United States, officials in both countries said.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said the operation had been conducted in close consultation with the new Venezuelan government.

item.title: An Honored Canadian Journalist on the Importance of Investigative Reporting
item.description: Our Times colleague Susanne Craig was back home in Canada to be honored for her extraordinary career, which began in Calgary, Alberta, her hometown.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.category: Lucky Loser: How Donald Trump Squandered His Father's Fortune and Created the Illusion of Success (Book)

item.title: Brazilians Revive a World Cup Ritual to Cheer on Their National Team
item.description: After years of dampened enthusiasm about the World Cup, Brazilians are excited about their national team again — and they’re showing it by painting the streets.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and Dado Galdieri
item.media:credit: Dado Galdieri for The New York Times

item.title: At the Canadian Screen Awards, the Industry Steps Outside Hollywood’s Shadow
item.description: No longer content with being just “Hollywood North,” the Canadian screen industry is asserting itself and telling its own stories.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Cole Burston
item.media:credit: Cole Burston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The actor Robbie Graham-Kunz speaking with fans outside the 14th Canadian Screen Awards in Toronto.

item.title: Canada Has Its Elbows Up and Arms Wide Open at the World Cup
item.description: Facing President Trump’s threats, many Canadians have taken a defensive stance to their neighbor. But now Canada is playing co-host with the United States and Mexico, and welcoming the world.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans celebrating a goal to tie the game during a public viewing of the match on Friday in Toronto.

item.title: World Cup Ticket Prices in Mexico City Too Expensive for Most Fans
item.description: The opening game in Mexico City took place amid weeks of protests from groups including teachers’ unions complaining about pay and pensions.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans sing the national anthem at Estadio Azteca in Mexico City before the opening match of the FIFA World Cup on Thursday.

item.title: As World Cup Begins, Mexico City Becomes a Global Stage
item.description: Supporters of the national team gathered inside and outside the stadium for the opening of the games, celebrating the start of a tournament years in the making.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans outside the Mexico City stadium known colloquially as Estadio Azteca before the opening match of the World Cup on Thursday.

item.title: U.S. Blocks Deal by Florida-based Vanguard Energy to Supply Fuel to Cuba
item.description: The deal to ship 250,000 barrels of fuel to Cuba could have eased an energy crisis. But the Trump administration says Vanguard Energy lacks the authorization to proceed.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:credit: Norlys Perez/Reuters
item.media:description: A blackout in Havana on Wednesday.

item.title: Security Is Tight as a Mexico Win Opens a Historic World Cup
item.description: As the largest World Cup kicked off in Mexico City on Thursday, the security around Estadio Azteca was strict, as law enforcement officers held off protesters.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Cesar Rodriguez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soccer fans in Mexico City encountered tight security on Thursday as Mexico and South Africa prepared to kick of the first of 104 World Cup matches.

item.title: Toronto Police Officer Fatally Shot While Investigating U.S. Consulate Shooting
item.description: The officer was shot on Thursday while investigating the firing of a gun outside the U.S. consulate in March, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Frank Gunn/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Police officers outside the United States Consulate in Toronto on March 10. Shots were fired at the building on that morning, the police said.

item.title: Thousands Protest Outside World Cup Opening Ceremony in Mexico City
item.description: At least 18,000 people protested on Thursday as the capital hosted the first match of the tournament, though the demonstrations appeared to be largely peaceful.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib, David Shortell, Cyntia Barrera Díaz and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Striking teachers shut down traffic along Paseo de la Reforma, one of Mexico City’s main thoroughfares, last week ahead of the World Cup.

item.title: Why Mexico’s President Will Not Attend the World Cup Opening Match
item.description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico plans to intentionally skip the entire tournament, breaking a decades-long tradition of sports diplomacy.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Quetzalli Nicte-Ha/Reuters
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico with Yolett Cervantes Cuaquehua, a young Indigenous woman who won a national contest held by the Mexican government to claim Ms. Sheinbaum’s World Cup ticket to the opening game.

item.title: This Is Mexico and Canada’s World Cup, Too. Don’t Expect Unity.
item.description: Mexico and Canada have faced significant recent tension in their relations with the United States. But in their historic World Cup bid, the three countries promoted teamwork.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mexico City’s iconic stadium will host the opener for a record third time, after undergoing a near $200 million makeover for this World Cup and increasing its capacity to 87,500 seats.

item.title: U.S. Officials Told Colombia to Cancel President’s Meeting With Mamdani
item.description: The State Department canceled President Gustavo Petro’s visa last year after he attended a pro-Palestinian rally in Manhattan. He had planned to attend a forum led by Mayor Mamdani of New York.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Edward Wong and Jeffery C. Mays
item.media:credit: Angela Weiss/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Gustavo Petro of Colombia during a United Nations Security Council meeting in New York on Wednesday.

item.title: Hot Cars and Stolen Crypto: A Canadian Teen Pocketed Millions, Prosecutors Say
item.description: A case illustrates both the low-hanging opportunities new financial technologies offer to scammers and the downside of speeding in a Rolls-Royce.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Saul Martinez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The C. Clyde Atkins U.S. Courthouse in Miami. Trenton Johnston, a Canadian, pleaded guilty in federal court to one count of conspiracy to commit money laundering.

item.title: Canada Moves to Ban Social Media Use for Youth Under 16
item.description: The country’s previous attempt to get tech companies to shelter young users failed amid heavy criticism from civil liberty groups.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Fred Thornhill/Reuters
item.media:description: People holding up cellphones at a concert in Toronto.

item.title: Trump Says He’s ‘Not Looking to Renew’ Canada-Mexico Trade Deal
item.description: The president’s comments come at a crucial moment in talks among the three countries over the renewal of their free-trade agreement.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff, Ana Swanson and Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney with President Trump at the Group of 7 summit last year in Kananaskis, Canada.

item.title: On the Eve of the World Cup, U.S. Immigration Policy Turns Some Away
item.description: Some fans and participants hoping to enter the United States for the World Cup have complained that restrictive immigration rules have presented a roadblock.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Ahmad Al-Rubaye/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Iraqi soccer player Aymen Hussein, pictured in a mural near Baghdad wearing his No. 18 jersey, was temporarily detained by U.S. immigration officials before being allowed to enter the country for the World Cup.

item.title: Argentina’s World Cup Sticker Albums Are a Low-Tech Craze Immune to the Digital Revolution
item.description: World Cup sticker albums have captivated children and adults in Argentina, in a collecting fever that seems immune to the digital revolution.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola, Lucía Cholakian Herrera and Anita Pouchard Serra
item.media:credit: Anita Pouchard Serra for The New York Times
item.media:description: People exchanging World Cup stickers in Buenos Aires last month.
